ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ajwani Machi Tikka; closest defensible parent match is fish kebab food product (FOODON_03315595). Ajwani Machi Tikka is a spiced, marinated, grilled fish preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, grilled) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific code exists for ajwain-marinated grilled fish tikka in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional South Asian fish dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Ajwani Machi Tikka is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate of 4 to 6 grilled fish pieces, as commonly served at South Asian restaurants and food delivery outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy #15264) and Chicken tikka masala, restaurant-prepared (Foundation Foods) used as compositional anchors; marinade spice contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ajwani Machi Tikka exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled fish (dry heat, mixed species) as the protein, fat, and energy base, with carbohydrate and sodium adjusted upward to reflect yogurt marinade, ajwain, chilli, and salt contributions consistent with ICMR-NIN spiced fish preparations. Input record values (protein 17.5 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 4.0 g, energy 175 kcal, sodium 325 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained. Calcium 40 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 50 mg, saturated fat 2.5 g, trans fat 0.0 g, vitamin C 5.0 mg, dietary fibre 1.0 g, and sugar 1.0 g are also consistent with the adapted source profile and are retained.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on high-protein low-carbohydrate grilled fish preparation; University of Sydney GI Database consulted.
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted. No direct GI value exists for Ajwani Machi Tikka or grilled spiced fish tikka. With only 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (predominantly from yogurt marinade and spices) and 17.5 g protein per 100 g, the food generates a minimal glycemic response. An analogous GI of 40 (low category) is assigned based on the closest defensible analogue: plain low-fat yogurt (GI ~35–40, University of Sydney GI Database) which represents the primary carbohydrate-contributing component of the marinade. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.2, which is very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 325 mg per 100 g (650 mg per standard 200 g serving) represents approximately 28% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg, which warrants portion awareness for individuals with hypertension.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request minimal added salt during preparation, and rely on lemon juice and ajwain for flavour rather than salt or garam masala; the high protein content (17.5 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) are otherwise supportive of cardiovascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content at 4.0 g per 100 g with only 1.0 g sugar per 100 g, combined with high protein at 17.5 g per 100 g from fish, produces a minimal postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ajwani Machi Tikka is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; serve with non-starchy vegetables or salad rather than naan or white rice, use plain low-fat yogurt without added sugar in the marinade, and monitor total meal carbohydrate load from accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat at 2.5 g per 100 g, high lean protein from fish, and the presence of anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, ajwain) support a cardioprotective dietary pattern; sodium at 325 mg per 100 g requires monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ajwani Machi Tikka is a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal oil and salt; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose omega-3-rich fish such as salmon or mackerel for the preparation to maximise EPA and DHA benefit, and limit the serving to 150 g to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric curcumin, ginger gingerols, ajwain thymol) and potential omega-3 content from fish may reduce joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from this dish when prepared with omega-3-rich fish such as salmon; those sensitive to nightshades or capsaicin should request reduced chilli powder, and added salt should be minimised to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajwani Machi Tikka is a South Asian-style grilled fish dish popular in Singapore's Indian and Pakistani restaurant scene, served commonly through food delivery platforms. The dish features firm white fish fillets — typically dory, snapper, or salmon — marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture containing ajwain (carom seeds), turmeric, red chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, garam masala, and oil. The marinated fish is skewered and cooked in a tandoor oven or over an open flame until the exterior is lightly charred and the interior is moist and flaky. The name 'Machi' derives from the Hindi and Urdu word for fish, while 'Ajwani' refers to the dominant spice ajwain, which imparts a distinctive thyme-like, slightly pungent aroma and aids digestion. Nutritionally, Ajwani Machi Tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 175 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 10.0 g fat, and only 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The low carbohydrate and high protein profile makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, and the low saturated fat content (2.5 g/100 g) supports cardiovascular health. Sodium at 325 mg per 100 g requires portion awareness for individuals with hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice blend — particularly turmeric and ginger — adds functional health value. A standard serving is approximately 200 g (one plate of 4–6 pieces), providing 350 kcal and 35 g protein. The dish is halal and gluten-free, making it accessible to a broad dietary audience in Singapore's multicultural food environment.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ajwani Machi Tikka exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy grilled fish (dry heat) as the primary compositional anchor, with marinade contributions estimated using ICMR-NIN spiced fish preparation data. The input record values are consistent with this adapted estimate and have been retained. Confidence is medium because the exact recipe formulation (fish species, oil quantity, marinade ratio) varies by restaurant and is not standardised in any approved source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for ajwain-marinated grilled fish tikka is not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN at the level of this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific spiced fish preparation is not reported in the adapted source entries consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific preparation in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN) as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a grilled fish preparation without hydrogenated fats, consistent with USDA FoodData Central grilled fish data. 5. Glycemic index is set to 40 (low category) as an analogous estimate based on plain low-fat yogurt (GI ~35–40, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the primary carbohydrate-contributing component of the marinade. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = 3.2 (very low). 6. The input record describes the cuisine as 'Turkish' but the dish name, spice profile (ajwain, turmeric, garam masala, ginger-garlic paste), and preparation method (tandoor) are unambiguously South Asian (Indian/Pakistani subcontinental). The cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian (South Asian / Subcontinental)' with country of origin noted as Singapore (South Asian diaspora cuisine). 7. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, data_source: grabfood) have been retained as they are consistent with the adapted USDA/ICMR-NIN estimate; the grabfood source is not used as primary evidence per Veritas source policy. 8. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'approximate') to comply with the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
